title: Poisson Boundary, Liouville Property and Asymptotic Geometry of Linear Groups
speaker: Anna Erschler
abstract:
The Poisson-Furstenberg boundary  is a measure space that describes asymptotics of infinite trajectories of random walks. The boundary is non-trivial if and only if the defining  measure admits non-constant bounded harmonic functions. 
The origin of the notion of Poisson boundary goes back to works of Blackwell, Feller and Doob, and in the case of random walks on groups to that of Dynkin-Malyutov and Furstenberg. Furstenberg (1963) provides a description of the boundary for semi-simple Lie groups (for measures absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure). An analogous description holds in discrete setting (Furstenberg; Kaimanovich). 
The last few decades brought a signifiant progress in understanding of  Poisson boundaries of groups. However, in the very classical case of linear groups even a basic question of boundary triviality or non-triviality remains open. We will speak about  partial progress and  a new conjectural description of this property for linear groups, various phenomena  for the action on the boundary and the connection to other geometric properties of solvable linear groups. We  plan to  discuss many open problems about geometry of linear groups.

proof_stages.label: Definitions of harmonic functions and Poisson boundary
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines mu-harmonic functions on a finitely generated group, the Liouville property as absence of nonconstant bounded harmonic functions, random walks driven by mu, and the Poisson boundary via bounded harmonic functions or shift components of path space.

proof_stages.label: Classical Liouville and non-Liouville criteria
proof_stages.summary: Classical results are reviewed: abelian and nilpotent or virtually nilpotent groups are Liouville in broad settings, nonamenable groups have nontrivial bounded harmonic functions, and Avez entropy gives a zero-entropy criterion equivalent to boundary triviality under finite-entropy hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Amenable exponential growth and the linear-group reduction problem
proof_stages.summary: After the entropy landscape, the focus narrows to amenable groups of exponential growth and then to finitely generated linear groups, where Tits alternative reduces the unresolved Liouville question to virtually solvable linear groups.

proof_stages.label: Known solvable linear benchmark classes
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls solvable linear classes already understood—nilpotent, polycyclic/metabelian, and wreath-product examples—to show that many cases are known but the general virtually solvable linear case remains open.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to two-by-two valid blocks
proof_stages.summary: For a d-by-d linear group in triangular form, the speaker associates to each valid pair of indices a two-by-two upper-triangular block and states the reduction theorem: the original random walk is Liouville exactly when all associated block walks are Liouville.

proof_stages.label: Positive-characteristic classification by Krull dimension
proof_stages.summary: In positive characteristic the two-by-two block problem is completely characterized by the relevant Krull dimension, with low dimensions yielding Liouville behavior and dimensions at least three yielding nontrivial boundary.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-zero polynomial obstruction and entropy method
proof_stages.summary: For characteristic zero the remaining hard case is tied to a Laurent-polynomial divisibility question; excluding cyclotomic monomial-difference obstructions should give the needed entropy lower bounds and hence the desired boundary classification.

proof_stages.label: Geometric consequences and open asymptotic questions
proof_stages.summary: The same polynomial and entropy issues are connected to asymptotic geometry of linear groups, including isoperimetric profiles and unresolved quasi-isometry or commensurability questions for nilpotent and exponential-growth solvable groups.

examples.name: Cyclotomic obstruction p = x - 1
examples.note: Taking p = x - 1 shows that q(x^c) can remain divisible by p for every c, forcing the non-cyclotomic/non-monomial-difference hypothesis in the polynomial question.
examples.engagement_reason: he verifies the cyclotomic obstruction by showing p=x−1 divides x^c−1 for every c
